intro: The pivotal question between Cohere and Bump.sh is how much of your setup stays portable: Cohere carries medium lock-in and Bump.sh carries low lock-in. Cohere is LLM API focused on enterprise text, embeddings, and RAG. Bump.sh is API documentation and change-tracking hub for OpenAPI/AsyncAPI schemas. Pick Cohere when the job is api gateway and rate limiting and you accept medium lock-in; pick Bump.sh when it is api gateway and rate limiting and you accept low lock-in. The data we have shows Cohere at mostly transparent pricing and docs with a polished developer experience, and Bump.sh at mostly transparent pricing and docs with a polished developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Cohere is a poor fit for internal, simple projects, and Bump.sh is a poor fit for internal, simple projects. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list. If your team already lives inside the ecosystem one of them assumes, that default usually wins — migration is cheap to start and expensive later.
quick_take: Cohere is for LLM API focused on enterprise text; Bump.sh is for API documentation and change-tracking hub; decide based on how portable the setup must stay.
verdict_a: Choose Cohere if some ecosystem coupling is acceptable and the work maps to LLM API focused on enterprise text, embeddings, and RAG.
verdict_b: Choose Bump.sh if day-to-day developer ergonomics are a priority and the work lines up with API documentation and change-tracking hub for OpenAPI/AsyncAPI schemas.

faq.a: Cohere starts at $4.00 on a subscription model; Bump.sh starts at $50 on a subscription model. That makes the sticker comparison depend on usage — a low entry price can get expensive once volume ramps, so price at your actual workload.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Cohere to Bump.sh?
faq.a: Migration is possible in either direction. Cohere carries medium lock-in and Bump.sh carries low lock-in, so plan for meaningful but not blocking rework. Run both in parallel before you fully cut over.

Both Cohere and Bump.sh rate the same on developer experience (5/5). The decision on DX then comes down to taste — which CLI, UI, or workflow matches your team's habits. A short side-by-side trial is the quickest way to tell.

faq.q: Is Bump.sh a good alternative to Cohere?
faq.a: Yes — Bump.sh is a reasonable alternative to Cohere for api gateway and rate limiting. The practical differences are subscription-vs-subscription billing and medium-vs-low lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Bump.sh as a credible swap.
